title: Doellingeria scabra
intro: Doellingeria scabra, commonly known as rough-leaved ragwort or rough-leaved coneflower, is a perennial plant species belonging to the sunflower family (Asteraceae). The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with potential health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant activities, which may help alleviate symptoms of various conditions such as fever, wounds, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Doellingeria scabra include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant has been used to prepare teas, infusions, and tinctures, with the dried leaves and flowers often being used in herbal medicine to support immune function and overall well-being.
taxonomy: Doellingeria scabra is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It belongs to the genus Doellingeria and the tribe Senecioneae. The species is also known as rough-leaved false goldenrod. Doellingeria scabra is native to North America, specifically in the northeastern part of the continent. This plant is a perennial herb that grows in a variety of habitats, including wetlands and woodland edges.
uses: Doellingeria scabra, also known as rough false goldenrod, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's leaves and stems are rich in flavonoids, which have been found to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Traditionally, the plant was used to treat respiratory issues, such as coughs and colds, as well as digestive problems. The plant's roots were also used to treat fever and skin conditions. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy of Doellingeria scabra for medicinal uses.
